title: Drift (Flutter SQLite ORM)
objectives: Understand Drift via a real-life analogy
See how it turns SQLite into a typed reactive DB
Write typed schema, migrations, DAOs, streams
Handle background isolates + platform channels correctly

sections.markdown:
**Think of Drift as a translator who speaks both English and Latin at the same time.**

- SQLite speaks **SQL** — a great, universal, decades-proven language. But writing raw SQL strings in Dart code means: typos crash at runtime, column names as strings (no auto-complete), no compile-time verification, painful refactors.
- Drift is the **translator**. You describe your tables in **Dart** (`class Users extends Table { IntColumn get id => integer().autoIncrement()(); ... }`); Drift generates typed classes, typed queries, typed streams. At the same time, if you need a complex SQL query, you write it in a **`.drift` file** and Drift PARSES it at build time — catches SQL errors before runtime.

- **The result:** you get SQLite's power (real SQL, real transactions, real indexes) with Dart's type safety (no runtime column-name typos, IDE auto-complete, safe refactors).

**Concretely:** Drift (formerly Moor) is a persistence library for Flutter and Dart. Under the hood it's SQLite; on top it's a typed Dart DSL + code generator + reactive streams. You define tables as Dart classes; run `build_runner`; get generated typed queries. `select(users).watch()` returns a `Stream<List<User>>` that re-emits whenever the `users` table changes anywhere in your app.

**Why it's the default Flutter SQL choice in 2026:**
- **Type-safe queries** — compile-time verification.
- **Reactive by default** — `.watch()` streams that auto-update the UI.
- **Full SQL when you need it** — `.drift` files for complex queries, SQL-file schema definitions.
- **Multi-platform** — mobile, desktop, web (via IndexedDB-backed sqlite.wasm).
- **Migration story** — schema-versioned migrations, tested with the migration test API.
- **Isolate support** — heavy queries off the UI thread.

**Trade-offs:** codegen requires `build_runner` (5-10s builds after schema edits); learning the DSL takes a session; verbose compared to raw SQL for simple queries. **All acceptable for the type safety + reactivity payoff.**

**Drift** (formerly **Moor**, renamed 2021) is a reactive typed persistence library for Flutter and Dart, built on SQLite. You define tables as Dart classes with `IntColumn`, `TextColumn`, etc.; a code generator produces typed data classes + typed query builders. Queries return `Future<T>` (one-shot) or `Stream<T>` (auto-updating). Supports `.drift` SQL files for complex queries — parsed at build time for compile-time SQL validation. Uses `sqlite3_flutter_libs` for native SQLite on mobile/desktop, `sqlite3.wasm` on web. Recommended default for Flutter apps needing relational persistence.
## What you get vs raw sqflite

rows: **`.drift` files** — parsed + verified at build time

sections.markdown: ## Sample program — a complete Drift database
## Migrations — the discipline every app eventually needs
## Common mistakes
**Mistake 1: Not running `build_runner` after schema changes.** Compile errors, stale generated code. Add `dart run build_runner watch --delete-conflicting-outputs` to your dev loop.
**Mistake 2: `NativeDatabase(file)` on the UI isolate.** Blocks the UI on heavy queries. Use `NativeDatabase.createInBackground(file)` — Drift handles the isolate.
**Mistake 3: Forgetting FK enforcement.** SQLite has `foreign_keys = OFF` by default. Enable in `beforeOpen`.
**Mistake 4: One giant DAO for the whole app.** Split by feature — `UserDao`, `OrderDao`, `SettingsDao`. Easier to test + navigate.
**Mistake 5: Not testing migrations.** Broken migration on a v-old user's device = data loss. Use the migration test API.
**Mistake 6: Reactive queries everywhere.** `.watch()` is great but not free — the stream is re-run on ANY change to the referenced tables. Narrow the query.
**Mistake 7: Skipping indexes.** Same rules as any SQL DB. Watch for slow queries; add composite indexes.
## Best practices
**1. `NativeDatabase.createInBackground()` on mobile.** Heavy queries off the UI isolate.
**2. `PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON` + `PRAGMA journal_mode = WAL`** in `beforeOpen`.
**3. DAOs per feature.** Not per table — per user-visible feature. `AuthDao`, `OrdersDao`, `SettingsDao`.
**4. Test migrations.** Every schemaVersion bump needs a test.
**5. `.drift` files for complex SQL.** Faster than expressing recursive CTEs in DSL.
**6. Type converters for enums, JSON, Duration.** Store as ints/strings; auto-convert.
**7. Use `.watchSingle()` for singletons.** Current user, current session — reactive by design.
**8. Riverpod / Bloc + Drift stream = offline-first UI.** Widget stream = DAO stream = SQLite file.
## Practice programs
**Practice 1 (Easy) — Users + Orders schema.** Full CRUD + `.watch()` list widget.
**Practice 2 (Medium) — DAO refactor.** Split into `UserDao` + `OrderDao`.
**Practice 3 (Medium) — JOIN + aggregation.** Top spenders query using join + groupBy.
**Practice 4 (Hard) — Migration.** Bump schemaVersion; add a column; write migration; add a migration test.
**Practice 5 (Hard) — Type converter.** Store `Status` enum + `List<String>` tags as strings; convert transparently.
**Practice 6 (Expert) — Riverpod + Drift stream.** `AsyncNotifier` reads a Drift stream; widget updates on any DB change.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What is Drift?**

**A:** Drift is a persistence library for Flutter + Dart, built on SQLite. You define tables as Dart classes; a code generator produces typed data classes + typed query builders. Queries return `Future<T>` or **`Stream<T>` for reactive UI**. Supports `.drift` SQL files for complex queries, verified at BUILD time. Uses native SQLite on mobile/desktop, `sqlite3.wasm` on web. **Compared to raw sqflite:** Drift adds type safety, reactive streams, migration testing, and DAOs — at the cost of a codegen step. **Compared to Hive/Isar:** Drift is SQL-based (relational); Hive/Isar are NoSQL. **When to pick Drift:** you want relational data + JOINs + queries + type safety + reactive streams — the modern default for Flutter local DBs.

**Q3: What are reactive queries in Drift?**

**A:** Queries have `.watch()` (returns `Stream`) as well as `.get()` (returns `Future`). The stream **auto-emits** whenever ANY of the tables the query references changes anywhere in the app. Example: `select(users).watch()` emits when `users` is inserted/updated/deleted. UI subscribes via `StreamBuilder` or Riverpod's `StreamProvider`. **Result:** offline-first UI with zero manual invalidation. Local DB change → stream emits → widget rebuilds. **Under the hood:** Drift tracks which tables each query touches; on write, notifies affected streams. **Compared to REST/HTTP:** normally your UI polls or invalidates cache manually. With Drift + a repository pattern, the DB is the source of truth; UI observes it via streams. **This is the killer feature.**

**Q4: How do you handle migrations?**

**A:** `@DriftDatabase` has `schemaVersion` (an int) and `MigrationStrategy`. `onCreate` runs on first install (`m.createAll()`). `onUpgrade(m, from, to)` runs when the app opens an older DB. Write conditional migration logic:
```dart
if (from < 2) await m.addColumn(users, users.createdAt);
if (from < 3) await customStatement('ALTER TABLE ...');
```
**Testing:** Drift provides a migration test API — load the old schema, insert sample data, run onUpgrade, assert data is preserved. **Critical because:** silent migration bugs corrupt users' data on updates; there's often no recovery. **Rule:** every schemaVersion bump has a test; run before every release. Drift's `drift_dev` also has a `SchemaSteps` API for even more rigorous testing.

**Q5: What is a DAO in Drift?**

**A:** A **DAO (Data Access Object)** is a class that owns queries for a subset of tables. `@DriftAccessor(tables: [Users, Orders])` — the DAO can query users + orders. Encourages separation of concerns: `UserDao` owns user queries, `OrderDao` owns orders. The main `@DriftDatabase` declares `daos: [UserDao, OrderDao]`; then `db.userDao.watchAllUsers()`. **Benefits:** clean separation, easier testing (test DAOs individually with a fake DB), better navigation in a large codebase. **Comparable to:** Room's `@Dao` in Android. **Best practice:** DAOs per FEATURE, not per table — one DAO can own multiple related tables. A user-facing feature typically maps to one DAO. Some tiny apps skip DAOs and put all queries on the main database class; that's fine at small scale.

**Q6: When should Drift work move to a background isolate?**

**A:** Complex JOINs or transactions on large tables can block the UI thread. Drift makes background isolates trivial with `NativeDatabase.createInBackground(file)` — Drift runs SQLite in a background isolate; queries return via ports; UI never blocks. **Rule:** use `createInBackground` on mobile by DEFAULT. The overhead is tiny; the UI-smoothness win is real. **Advanced:** for truly heavy workloads (bulk imports, migrations of large data), spawn a dedicated computation isolate + Drift's isolate API. **Comparison:** without Drift's isolate helper, you'd manually manage `Isolate.spawn` + `SendPort` + serialization — Drift wraps it for you.

**Q7: Drift vs sqflite vs Isar for a Flutter app?**

**A:** **sqflite** — thin wrapper over native SQLite. Raw SQL strings. Fine for a small app with 2-3 tables where the ceremony of codegen isn't worth it. Battle-tested, minimal footprint. **Drift** — SQLite + typed Dart DSL + reactive streams + migrations. Modern default for medium-to-large Flutter apps needing SQL. Trade-off: codegen (build_runner). **Isar** — NoSQL, super-fast, reactive queries, no SQL. Great when your data is document-shaped or when raw performance matters over SQL. Trade-off: no SQL knowledge transferable, no JOINs. **Rule:** SQL-shaped data + type safety + reactive UI → Drift. Simple SQL-shaped data + minimal deps → sqflite. Document-shaped or performance-critical → Isar. Many Flutter apps use Drift for the main relational data + Hive for KV — a fine combination.

## Real-world usage

- **Many production Flutter apps** — the go-to SQL choice.
- **Reactive + offline-first apps** — Riverpod/Bloc + Drift stream = magic.
- **`drift_dev`** — active development, monthly releases.
- **Simon Binder** (drift maintainer) is highly responsive on GitHub.
- **Cross-platform** — same codebase runs on iOS, Android, macOS, Windows, Linux, web (via sqlite3.wasm).
- **NOT for** — pure KV workloads (use Hive), extreme performance in the NoSQL space (use Isar), or apps too small to justify codegen.

## Advanced notes

- **`.drift` SQL files** — write complex queries in real SQL; Drift parses + type-checks.
- **`TypeConverter<T, DB>`** — enums, JSON, DateTime, Duration — automatic.
- **`@UseRowClass(MyClass)`** — use your own Dart class instead of the generated data class.
- **`drift_dev schema`** — dump / diff schemas across versions for tracked migrations.
- **Isolate connection** — send DB across isolate boundaries via `DriftIsolate`.
- **Web support** — `sqlite3.wasm` + IndexedDB persistence.
- **`sqlite_async`** — competing package by Journey Apps; more geared to sync workflows.
- **`brick_sqlite`** (in the offline-first library) — Drift-based offline persistence with sync.
- **`riverpod_generator` + Drift** — perfect combination for reactive state.
- **`drift_dev` linter** — catches Drift-specific issues.
- **Backup + export** — Drift's `NativeDatabase` uses a plain SQLite file; back it up like any file.
- **Column defaults** — `withDefault(currentDateAndTime)`, `Constant(0)` etc.
- **Foreign keys** — `references(Users, #id, onDelete: KeyAction.cascade)` for cascade behavior.
- **`companion.insert(...)`** vs `companion.custom(...)` — different for defaults vs required fields.

flashcards.back: SQLite + typed Dart DSL + reactive .watch() streams + migrations + codegen. Modern Flutter SQL default.
build_runner code generation — column refs are Dart properties; typos fail at compile time.
.watch() returns Stream; auto-emits when referenced tables change anywhere in the app.
@DriftDatabase schemaVersion + MigrationStrategy.onUpgrade with per-version logic. Test with the migration test API.
@DriftAccessor per feature — clean separation, better testing. UserDao, OrdersDao.
NativeDatabase.createInBackground(file) — Drift handles the isolate; UI never blocks.
Drift = SQL + typed + reactive. Hive = pure KV. Isar = NoSQL + queries + fast. Pick by shape of your data.
